tier_2_handbook.definition: Occult primary tumors, also termed cancers of unknown primary (CUP), are defined as histologically confirmed metastatic malignancies for which a primary anatomical site cannot be identified after a standard pretreatment evaluation [1,2]. These tumors are characterized by early dissemination, aggressive clinical behavior, and unpredictable patterns of metastasis [3]. The median overall survival (OS) for patients with CUP is 8 to 12 months, although select patients with favorable prognostic subsets may achieve median OS in the range of 12 to 36 months [2,4,5]. The majority of CUP cases are epithelial in origin, with the most frequent histologic subtypes being adenocarcinoma (well- or moderately differentiated, 60%; poorly differentiated, 25%), squamous cell carcinoma (SCC, 5%), undifferentiated carcinoma (5%), and neuroendocrine tumors (5%) [1,2,27]. The NCCN Guidelines for Occult Primary focus on two major pathologic diagnoses: adenocarcinoma or carcinoma not otherwise specified, and squamous cell carcinoma; neuroendocrine tumors and head and neck occult primaries are managed according to their respective disease-specific guidelines [OCC-1, OCC-2, Discussion MS-2].

subtypes.name: Adenocarcinoma (well- or moderately differentiated)
subtypes.description: The most common subtype, accounting for approximately 60% of CUP cases. Tumors exhibit glandular differentiation on light microscopy.
subtypes.key_associations: Often associated with unfavorable prognosis; may present with multiple visceral metastases; responsive to platinum/taxane-based empiric chemotherapy; considered for site-specific therapy if IHC or molecular profiling suggests a primary site.

subtypes.name: Adenocarcinoma (poorly differentiated)
subtypes.description: Represents approximately 25% of CUP cases. Tumors show limited glandular differentiation and higher nuclear grade.
subtypes.key_associations: More chemosensitive than well-differentiated adenocarcinoma; response rates to cisplatin-based regimens may be higher; some cases harbor extragonadal germ cell features with improved outcomes.

subtypes.name: Squamous cell carcinoma (SCC)
subtypes.description: Accounts for about 5% of CUP cases. Characterized by squamous differentiation on histology and cytokeratin 5/6, p63/p40 positivity.
subtypes.key_associations: Better prognosis than adenocarcinoma (3-year survival 41.6% vs 3.5%) [2]; often presents with cervical, axillary, or inguinal lymphadenopathy; p16/HPV and EBV ISH testing may help localize primary site (oropharynx, cervix, anus, etc.).

subtypes.name: Undifferentiated carcinoma
subtypes.description: Comprises approximately 5% of CUP cases. Tumors lack recognizable differentiation on light microscopy.
subtypes.key_associations: Highly responsive to cisplatin-based combination chemotherapy; may be treated with germ cell tumor protocols if midline distribution; genomic profiling may identify actionable alterations [1,2,27].

subtypes.name: Neuroendocrine tumors
subtypes.description: Account for approximately 5% of CUP cases. Include well-differentiated neuroendocrine tumors (carcinoids) and poorly differentiated neuroendocrine carcinomas (PDNEC).
subtypes.key_associations: Favorable prognostic subset; PDNEC are chemosensitive to platinum/etoposide regimens; well-differentiated tumors are managed per NCCN Guidelines for Neuroendocrine and Adrenal Tumors.

tier_2_handbook.molecular_pathogenesis: CUP tumors harbor multiple chromosomal abnormalities and overexpression of various oncogenes and tumor suppressor genes. Common genomic alterations include deletions in tumor suppressor genes p53 (49.6%), CDKN2A (19.0%), and NOTCH1 (14.1%), as well as activation of oncogenes KRAS (23.4%), FGFR4 (14.9%), and PIK3CA (10.7%) [33]. Other frequently altered genes include EGFR, MET, JAK3, BRAF, c-kit/PDGFR (PDGFRα or PDGFRβ), RAS, BCL2, HER2, and TP53 [9,28-30]. Activation of angiogenesis genes is observed in 50%–89% of CUP tumors, oncogene overexpression in 10%–30%, epithelial-to-mesenchymal transition marker elevation in 16%, activation of hypoxia-related proteins in 25%, and intracellular signaling molecule activation in 20%–35% [2]. The BRD4-NUT oncogene, resulting from the chromosomal translocation t(15;19), has been identified in children and young adults with carcinoma of midline structures and unclear primary sites [1,31,32]. Chromosomal instability has been suggested as a possible cause or prognostic factor for more aggressive presentations of CUP [2,34]. Both KRAS activation and CDKN2A deletion are associated with poor prognosis [33]. Comprehensive genomic profiling using next-generation sequencing can identify potentially actionable alterations in 30%–85% of patients, including mutations in BRAF V600E, NTRK1/2/3 fusions, RET fusions, HER2 amplification, and markers of microsatellite instability (MSI) and high tumor mutational burden (TMB-H) [24,74,78-82]. The tumor mutational landscape in CUP may guide site-specific therapy and biomarker-driven treatment strategies [OCC-B, Discussion MS-7].
tier_2_handbook.epidemiology: CUP has an average age at diagnosis of 60 to 75 years and accounts for 2% to 9% of all malignant tumors [2,8]. It is among the 10 most frequently diagnosed cancers in developed countries [2,9]. In the United States, an estimated 67,800 cases of cancer from other and unspecified primary sites are expected to be diagnosed in 2026, representing approximately 3.2% of all US cancers [10,11]. Deaths from these tumors are projected to reach 54,310 in 2026 [10]. The incidence of CUP diagnoses has been decreasing, likely due to improved diagnostic techniques that detect primary tumors, but no improvement in survival has been reported [12,13]. A study using the Swedish Family-Cancer Database revealed that 2.8% of occult primary cases are familial (parent and offspring both diagnosed), and CUP is associated with the occurrence of lung, kidney, and colorectal cancers in families, suggesting these are often the primary sites [14]. A latent primary cancer may emerge during the disease course in 20%–50% of cases, but postmortem examination fails to identify the primary in 20%–50% of patients [8,15,16]. An epidemiologic correlation between smoking and CUP risk has been observed: individuals who smoked >20 cigarettes per day had a strong association (multivariable adjusted hazard ratio [HR] 1.45; 95% CI 1.09–1.94; P trend = .02) for those who smoked ≥40 years compared to never smokers [17]. CUP occurs across racial and ethnic groups, but specific race-stratified incidence data were not provided in the source. The median OS for the majority of patients (about 80%) is 3 to 10 months, with adenocarcinomas and undifferentiated tumors having worse prognosis than SCC (3.5% vs 41.6% 3-year survival) [2,21].

risk_factors.detail: Strong association between smoking and risk of developing CUP. Frequency (>20 cigarettes per day) and duration (≥40 years) increase risk. Multivariable adjusted HR 1.45 (95% CI 1.09–1.94; P trend = .02) for those who smoked ≥40 years compared to never smokers.
risk_factors.strength: Strong (statistically significant in literature review, shown in multiple analyses)

risk_factors.detail: 2.8% of CUP cases are familial (parent and offspring both affected). CUP is associated with familial occurrence of lung, kidney, and colorectal cancers, suggesting these may be the primary sites.
risk_factors.strength: Moderate (based on Swedish Family-Cancer Database analysis)

risk_factors.detail: Older age is an unfavorable prognostic factor, but also a risk factor for developing CUP given median age at diagnosis of 60-75 years. However, the source does not explicitly state age as a causative risk factor; it is more a demographic characteristic.
risk_factors.strength: Not explicitly quantified as a risk factor for development (prognostic factor)

risk_factors.detail: Male gender assigned at birth is associated with worse prognosis and may influence incidence, but source does not provide specific incidence by gender. In prognostic models, male gender is associated with worse survival.
risk_factors.strength: Not explicitly quantified for risk of developing CUP (prognostic factor)

clinical_features.typical_presentation: Cancer of unknown primary (CUP) typically presents in patients aged 60–75 years with metastases from an unidentified primary site. The most common sites of involvement are the liver, lungs, bones, and lymph nodes, and more than 50% of patients present with multiple metastatic sites [2], [18]. Approximately 80% of patients have an unfavorable prognosis with median overall survival (OS) of 3–10 months; however, a favorable subset (approximately 20%) may achieve median OS of 12–36 months [2], [4], [5]. Classic presentations include cervical lymphadenopathy from squamous cell carcinoma (SCC), axillary adenopathy in women suggesting occult breast cancer, midline nodal distribution in young men suggesting germ cell tumors, and peritoneal carcinomatosis in women suggesting ovarian primary. Symptoms are often nonspecific and include fatigue, weight loss, pain at metastatic sites, and organ-specific complaints such as dyspnea (pleural effusion), abdominal distension (ascites), or bone pain. The diagnosis is made after standard pretreatment evaluation fails to identify a primary tumor [1], [2].

symptoms.name: Weight loss
symptoms.detail: Unintentional weight loss is common, often reflecting advanced disease and catabolic state.
symptoms.frequency: Frequent but not quantified in source.
symptoms.alarm: May indicate aggressive biology or extensive disease.

symptoms.name: Fatigue
symptoms.detail: Generalized weakness and fatigue are among the most common nonspecific symptoms.
symptoms.frequency: Very common, present in majority of patients.
symptoms.alarm: Persistent fatigue may signal progressive disease.

symptoms.name: Pain
symptoms.detail: Pain localized to the site of metastasis (e.g., bone pain, abdominal pain, chest pain).
symptoms.frequency: Common, especially with bone or liver involvement.
symptoms.alarm: Bone pain with potential fracture or impending cord compression requires urgent evaluation.

symptoms.name: Dyspnea
symptoms.detail: Shortness of breath related to pleural effusion, lung nodules, or lymphangitic spread.
symptoms.frequency: Occurs in presentations with chest involvement or pleural effusion.
symptoms.alarm: Rapid onset of dyspnea may indicate massive pleural effusion or pulmonary embolism.

symptoms.name: Abdominal distension
symptoms.detail: Abdominal swelling due to ascites or peritoneal carcinomatosis.
symptoms.frequency: Seen in peritoneal/ascites presentation, especially adenocarcinoma.
symptoms.alarm: Rapid accumulation may indicate aggressive disease.

symptoms.name: Neurologic deficits
symptoms.detail: Headache, seizures, focal deficits from brain metastases.
symptoms.alarm: Any new neurologic symptom requires urgent imaging.

signs.name: Palpable lymphadenopathy
signs.detail: Cervical, supraclavicular, axillary, or inguinal lymph node enlargement. May be firm, fixed, or matted.
signs.frequency: Common, especially in SCC head and neck or adenocarcinoma axillary presentation.

signs.name: Hepatomegaly
signs.detail: Enlarged liver due to metastatic deposits.

signs.name: Ascites
signs.detail: Abdominal fluid wave, shifting dullness, often with peritoneal carcinomatosis.

signs.name: Pleural effusion
signs.detail: Decreased breath sounds, dullness to percussion on chest exam.

signs.name: Bone tenderness
signs.detail: Pain on palpation over metastatic bone lesions.

signs.name: Skin lesions
signs.detail: Cutaneous metastases may rarely be the presenting sign; primary skin cancer must be excluded.
signs.frequency: Uncommon; complete skin exam is recommended for SCC.

atypical_or_missed: Spontaneously regressing lesions that may obscure primary site identification [OCC-1].
History of previously removed lesions that were not pathologically evaluated.
Presentation with only a single metastasis (e.g., isolated bone or brain lesion) leading to delayed diagnosis of CUP.
Midline nodal distribution in young adults that mimics germ cell tumor but ultimately diagnosed as CUP.
Occult primary breast cancer presenting with axillary adenopathy and negative mammogram; breast MRI may be required [OCC-3].
Peritoneal carcinomatosis in women with normal pelvic exam and imaging; ovarian primary may not be identified until surgery.

red_flags: Poor performance status (ECOG ≥2) is a strong independent predictor of worse survival [5].
Multiple organ involvement (liver, lung, bone) at presentation [2].
Bone lesions in weight-bearing areas with potential for pathologic fracture [OCC-10].
Rapid disease progression or symptomatic decline despite initial therapy.
Male gender assigned at birth and older age (≥65 years) are unfavorable prognostic factors [2].
Psychosocial distress: significant anxiety/depression due to uncertainty of diagnosis, which may impair treatment acceptance [OCC-1 footnote a].
Elevated LDH may indicate high tumor burden.

diagnostic.test: Complete history and physical (H&P) including breast, GU, pelvic, rectal, skin, and oral cavity exam
diagnostic.rationale: Identify potential primary sites; review past biopsies, removed lesions, spontaneously regressing lesions, and existing imaging.
diagnostic.key_findings: Unexplained lymphadenopathy, organomegaly, suspicious skin lesions, abnormal rectal/pelvic exam.

diagnostic.test: Core needle biopsy (preferred) or FNA with cell block of most accessible metastatic lesion
diagnostic.rationale: Obtain adequate tissue for histologic diagnosis and ancillary studies.
diagnostic.key_findings: Adenocarcinoma (60%), poorly differentiated adenocarcinoma (25%), SCC (5%), undifferentiated carcinoma (5%), neuroendocrine tumors (5%) [1], [2].

diagnostic.test: Complete blood count (CBC) and comprehensive metabolic panel (CMP)
diagnostic.rationale: Baseline assessment; detect anemia, liver or renal dysfunction, electrolyte abnormalities.
diagnostic.key_findings: May reveal anemia, elevated LFTs, hypercalcemia.

diagnostic.rationale: Prognostic marker; elevated LDH associated with higher tumor burden.

diagnostic.test: Chest/abdomen/pelvis CT with contrast (or MRI with contrast if contraindicated; FDG-PET/CT alternative)
diagnostic.rationale: Comprehensive imaging to identify primary tumor and extent of metastases.
diagnostic.key_findings: May detect occult primary (e.g., lung nodule, pancreatic mass, ovarian mass) and define metastatic burden.

diagnostic.rationale: To visualize potential primary sites in GI, GU, or respiratory tract if symptoms or IHC suggest.
Determine cell lineage (carcinoma, lymphoma, melanoma, germ cell, mesothelioma) and identify putative primary site.
diagnostic.key_findings: CK7/CK20 pattern, organ-specific markers (e.g., TTF-1, GATA3, CDX2, PSA, PAX8). Accuracy ~75% in identifying primary site [43].

diagnostic.test: In situ hybridization (ISH) for HPV (p16), EBV
diagnostic.rationale: HPV/p16 positivity suggests oropharyngeal SCC; EBV positivity suggests nasopharyngeal primary [OCC-11 footnote q].
diagnostic.key_findings: p16+ correlates with HPV ISH in >98% of head and neck SCC [52].

diagnostic.test: MSI/MMR testing (IHC for MMR or PCR for MSI)
diagnostic.rationale: Identify dMMR/MSI-H tumors that may respond to immune checkpoint inhibitors. Low prevalence in CUP (<2%) [84].
diagnostic.key_findings: MSI-H/dMMR status indicates potential benefit from pembrolizumab or dostarlimab.

diagnostic.rationale: Identify HER2-positive (IHC 3+) tumors eligible for fam-trastuzumab deruxtecan-nxki.
diagnostic.key_findings: HER2 3+ expression in a subset of CUP; ORR 61.3% with fam-trastuzumab deruxtecan in HER2+ solid tumors [201].

diagnostic.test: Biomarker testing using NGS (tumor tissue or cell-free DNA) for gene alterations and TMB
diagnostic.rationale: Identify actionable mutations (BRAF V600E, NTRK fusions, RET fusions, MSI-H, TMB-H) for targeted therapy or immunotherapy.
diagnostic.key_findings: Actionable alterations found in 30–85% of CUP depending on definition [24], [74], [80]; TMB-H (≥10 mut/Mb) identified in KEYNOTE-158 [190].

staging.test: Chest/abdomen/pelvis CT with contrast (or MRI) – as above
staging.rationale: Standard imaging to assess extent of disease and guide management.
Alternative if contrast contraindicated; may detect occult primary and additional metastases (sensitivity ~97% for cervical nodes) [91].
staging.indication: Contraindication to contrast, or when local/regional therapy is being considered (e.g., single metastasis).

staging.rationale: Evaluate for brain metastases in patients with neurologic symptoms or multiple sites of involvement.
staging.indication: Suspected brain metastases; recommended per NCCN CNS Cancers guidelines.

staging.test: Bone scan (or FDG-PET/CT plus CT bone windows)
staging.rationale: Detect bone metastases, especially if painful or potential pathologic fracture.
staging.indication: Bone pain, elevated alkaline phosphatase, or known bone lesion on CT.

staging.test: Mammogram (plus breast MRI and/or ultrasound if nondiagnostic and histopathologic evidence for breast cancer)
staging.rationale: Identify occult breast primary in patients with axillary, supraclavicular, mediastinal, or other metastatic patterns suggestive of breast cancer.
staging.indication: All patients with intact breast tissue (including gynecomastia) and adenocarcinoma or carcinoma NOS with clinical presentation compatible with breast origin.

staging.test: Serum tumor markers (PSA, CA-125, AFP, hCG, CA 19-9, CA 15-3 as clinically indicated)
staging.rationale: Elevations can suggest primary site (PSA for prostate, CA-125 for ovary, AFP/hCG for germ cell). Not diagnostic but may guide workup.
staging.indication: Per clinical presentation: PSA >40y; AFP/hCG for mediastinal/retroperitoneal mass <50y; CA-125 for peritoneal/ascites in females.

staging.test: Testicular ultrasound (if AFP/hCG elevated or clinical suspicion)

staging.rationale: Evaluate for anal or rectal primary.
To evaluate ovarian/primary peritoneal cancer, especially in females with peritoneal/ascites or elevated CA-125.
staging.indication: Females with peritoneal/ascites, inguinal nodes, or elevated CA-125.

biomarkers.test: NGS panel for gene alterations (including BRAF, NTRK, RET, MSI, TMB)
biomarkers.rationale: Identify targetable alterations for biomarker-driven therapy (dabrafenib/trametinib, entrectinib/larotrectinib/repotrectinib, selpercatinib, pembrolizumab).
biomarkers.when: After histologic diagnosis; recommended for all patients who are candidates for anti-cancer therapy [OCC-1].
biomarkers.mandatory: Yes – NGS (or other technique) and MSI/MMR testing and HER2 IHC are recommended as part of initial workup.

biomarkers.rationale: May identify patients likely to respond to immune checkpoint inhibitors, though not mandatory for pembrolizumab in dMMR/MSI-H or TMB-H.
biomarkers.when: Consider for patients with recurrent, progressive, or metastatic disease (per footnotes in OCC-B).

biomarkers.rationale: Screening for NTRK fusions; positive results should be confirmed with NGS [OCC-A footnote 2].
biomarkers.when: Consider as part of broad IHC testing.

biomarkers.rationale: Identify DPYD deficiency to prevent severe fluorouracil toxicity.
biomarkers.when: Before initiating fluorouracil- or capecitabine-based therapy.
biomarkers.mandatory: Not explicitly mandatory but referenced in OCC-B 1 of 14.

do_not_miss: Core needle biopsy with sufficient tissue for IHC, ISH, and molecular testing.
NGS biomarker testing (including TMB and MSI) and HER2 IHC – critical for identifying actionable targets.
HPV/p16 and EBV ISH in SCC to identify oropharyngeal or nasopharyngeal primary.
Breast imaging (mammogram ± MRI/ultrasound) in all patients with appropriate anatomy and clinical presentation.
PSA testing in males >40y with adenocarcinoma (except isolated liver/brain metastases).
AFP/hCG and testicular ultrasound in young males with mediastinal or retroperitoneal mass.
Gynecologic oncologist consult for females with peritoneal/ascites or elevated CA-125.
avoid: Tissue of origin gene expression profiling (GEP) assays are not recommended [OCC-1 footnote i].
Large panels of IHC markers (>10–12 stains) do not improve diagnostic accuracy and should be avoided [47].
Empiric broad tumor marker panels (CA-125, CA 19-9, CA 15-3) without specific clinical indication; these are not diagnostic and may mislead [OCC-1 footnote b].
Routine colonoscopy in absence of GI symptoms, positive fecal occult blood, or other clinical suspicion – low yield [123].
Exhaustive, time-consuming workup beyond initial tests without directing by clinical presentation and IHC results [OCC-1].

staging.system: No formal AJCC TNM staging system exists for Occult Primary (CUP). Instead, patients are stratified by prognostic groups (favorable vs unfavorable) based on clinical and pathologic factors.

t_stages.description: No primary tumor identified after standard initial evaluation. This is the defining feature of CUP. No T stage classification applicable.

n_stages.description: Lymph node involvement is a common clinical presentation, but no formal N staging for CUP. Clinical descriptions include cervical, supraclavicular, axillary, inguinal, mediastinal, and retroperitoneal nodal involvement.

m_stages.description: Distant metastasis is always present by definition (occult primary with metastatic disease). No specific M substages are used.

stage_groups.criteria: Includes patients with: single, small potentially resectable tumor; poorly differentiated carcinoma with midline nodal distribution; SCC involving cervical lymph nodes (2–5% of CUP); isolated inguinal adenopathy; poorly differentiated neuroendocrine carcinomas (PDNECs); women with papillary adenocarcinoma of peritoneal cavity; women with adenocarcinoma only in axillary nodes; men with blastic bone metastases and elevated PSA [2], [22], [26], [27]. Also patients with PS 0–1, low number of metastatic sites, low neutrophil-lymphocyte ratio [5].
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: These patients have a longer median OS (12–36 months) and may benefit from site-specific therapy or locoregional approaches.
stage_groups.five_yr_survival: Not explicitly provided for favorable group; 3-year survival for SCC is 41.6% vs 3.5% for adenocarcinoma/undifferentiated tumors [2].
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Potentially curative or disease control with site-specific therapy (e.g., breast, germ cell, head/neck algorithms).

stage_groups.criteria: Includes: male gender assigned at birth; age ≥65 years; poor PS (ECOG ≥2); multiple comorbidities; multiple organ metastases (liver, lung, bone); nonpapillary malignant ascites; peritoneal metastases; multiple cerebral metastases; adenocarcinoma with multiple lung/pleural or bone lesions; high neutrophil-lymphocyte ratio [2], [5], [22], [24].
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Median OS 3–10 months; empiric chemotherapy offers limited benefit. Treatment is palliative with focus on symptom control.
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Palliative; symptom control, clinical trial preferred, systemic therapy on individual basis.

stage_groups.group: Favorable subsets with specific primary identified by IHC/molecular profiling
stage_groups.criteria: Patients in whom a putative primary is identified (e.g., breast, colon, lung, ovary, prostate, germ cell) and treated per disease-specific guidelines.
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Outcomes align with the identified primary cancer; median OS may improve when site-specific therapy is used (e.g., 28.2 months vs 19 months in one RCT) [44].
stage_groups.five_yr_survival: Varies by primary; not specified for CUP.
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Site-specific therapy per NCCN disease-specific guidelines.

staging_pearls: CUP is not staged with AJCC; instead, prognostic stratification is used based on clinical factors, histology, and newly validated nomograms [5].
The Raghav nomogram (2021) incorporates gender, ECOG PS, histology, number of metastatic sites, and neutrophil-lymphocyte ratio to predict OS with concordance index of 0.71 [5].

Favorable prognosis subsets include specific clinical-pathologic entities (e.g., women with axillary nodes, men with blastic bone metastases and elevated PSA) that should be identified and treated accordingly.
Poor prognosis features (male, age ≥65, poor PS, multiple organ metastases, adenocarcinoma histology) define the majority of patients who are candidates for empiric chemotherapy or clinical trials.
Primary site may emerge months to years later; 20–50% never identified even at autopsy [8], [15], [16].
Detection of a primary site (via FDG-PET/CT or IHC) is associated with improved median OS (19.8 vs 8.5 months) in retrospective analysis [92].

management_principles.overview: Occult primary (cancer of unknown primary, CUP) is a heterogeneous group of histologically confirmed metastatic malignancies for which the primary site cannot be identified during standard pretreatment evaluation. These tumors are characterized by early dissemination, aggressiveness, and unpredictable metastatic patterns. Median survival is 8 to 12 months, though select patients with favorable subsets (e.g., single small resectable lesion, midline nodal distribution of poorly differentiated carcinoma, squamous cell carcinoma [SCC] involving cervical lymph nodes, isolated inguinal adenopathy [SCC], poorly differentiated neuroendocrine carcinomas, women with papillary adenocarcinoma of the peritoneal cavity, women with adenocarcinoma involving only axillary lymph nodes, men with blastic bone metastases and elevated PSA) have median overall survival (OS) ranging from 12 to 36 months [2,4,5]. The treatment philosophy stresses palliation and symptom control in the majority; systemic therapy is palliative and does not significantly improve long-term survival for disseminated disease. However, tailored locoregional treatments or specific systemic regimens (e.g., fluorouracil-based for suspected colon primary, cisplatin-based for suspected germ cell tumor) may provide clinical benefit and prolong survival in favorable subsets. Multimodal approaches integrating surgery, radiation therapy (RT), and systemic therapy are individualized based on histology (adenocarcinoma/carcinoma not otherwise specified vs. SCC), clinical presentation, workup findings (including biomarker results), and performance status (PS). Enrollment in clinical trials is strongly encouraged.

by_intent.patient_group: Favorable prognostic subsets: single small potentially resectable tumor; poorly differentiated carcinoma with midline nodal distribution; SCC cervical lymph nodes (constituting 2%–5% of occult primary cancers); isolated inguinal adenopathy (SCC); women with papillary adenocarcinoma of peritoneal cavity; women with adenocarcinoma involving only axillary lymph nodes; men with blastic bone metastases and elevated PSA; poorly differentiated neuroendocrine carcinomas.
by_intent.approach: Tailored locoregional treatments (surgery, RT) and/or specific systemic therapy regimens (e.g., cisplatin-based for germ cell, fluorouracil-based for colorectal profile) may achieve prolonged survival. For isolated axillary adenocarcinoma, treat per NCCN Guidelines for Breast Cancer. For mediastinal adenocarcinoma in patients <40 years, treat as poor-risk germ cell tumor per NCCN Guidelines for Testicular Cancer or Ovarian Cancer.

by_intent.patient_group: Disseminated metastases or unfavorable features (multiple organ involvement, poor PS, male gender at birth, age ≥65 years, multiple comorbidities, nonpapillary malignant ascites, peritoneal metastases, multiple cerebral metastases, adenocarcinoma with multiple lung/pleural or bone lesions).
by_intent.approach: Symptom control, clinical trial preferred, consideration of systemic therapy on an individual basis (symptomatic PS 1–2 or asymptomatic PS 0 with aggressive cancer). Specialized approaches (palliative thoracentesis/paracentesis, targeted therapies, novel RT). Psychosocial support and early end-of-life discussions.

management_principles.mdt_requirements: Communication between clinician and pathologist is essential for workup to direct immunohistochemistry (IHC) staining pattern to the clinical differential diagnosis. Multidisciplinary team should include medical oncology, surgical oncology, radiation oncology, pathology, radiology, and supportive/palliative care services. For patients with peritoneal/ascites, consultation with surgical or gynecologic oncologist as appropriate. For inguinal nodes, gynecologic oncologist consult (in those with uterus and/or ovaries present). For retroperitoneal mass, consider gynecologic oncologist consult if CA-125 elevated. For head and neck presentations, treatment per NCCN Guidelines for Head and Neck Cancers.
management_principles.performance_status_guide: Consider systemic therapy in symptomatic patients (PS 1–2) or asymptomatic patients (PS 0) with aggressive cancer. For patients with poor PS, prioritize symptom control, clinical trial, and supportive care. The use of intensive regimens like FOLFIRINOX, mFOLFIRINOX, or carboplatin/etoposide/paclitaxel is reserved for patients with PS ECOG 0–1 [34,36,37,33]. In patients with bone lesions and good PS, surgery for impending fracture and/or RT can be considered; for poor PS, RT alone is recommended.

management_pathways.title: Adenocarcinoma – Predominant and isolated cervical nodes / Head and neck
branching_basis: Histology: adenocarcinoma or carcinoma not otherwise specified

nodes.criteria: Additional workup per NCCN Guidelines for Head and Neck Cancers

options.label: Treat per NCCN Guidelines for Head and Neck Cancers
options.recommendation: Follow the Occult Primary pathway described in the NCCN Guidelines for Head and Neck Cancers [OCC-3, OCC-8].

nodes.criteria: Additional workup: neck/chest/abdomen/pelvis CT; appropriate IHC; mammogram (if breast tissue or gynecomastia); if nondiagnostic and histopathologic evidence for breast cancer, breast MRI and/or ultrasound; PSA >40 y (if prostate present)

options.label: Treat per NCCN Guidelines for Breast Cancer Screening and Diagnosis and NCCN Guidelines for Breast Cancer
options.recommendation: Based on workup findings, management is per breast cancer guidelines [OCC-3, OCC-8].

nodes.criteria: Additional workup: neck/chest/abdomen/pelvis CT; appropriate IHC; mammogram; if nondiagnostic and histopathologic evidence for breast cancer, breast MRI and/or ultrasound; PSA >40 y

options.label: Treat per NCCN Guidelines for Breast Cancer Screening and Diagnosis and NCCN Guidelines for Breast Cancer
options.recommendation: Management based on workup findings; if breast primary identified, treat per breast cancer guidelines [OCC-3, OCC-8].

options.label: Treat as poor-risk germ cell tumor per NCCN Guidelines for Testicular Cancer or germ cell tumor per NCCN Guidelines for Ovarian Cancer
options.recommendation: Additional workup includes beta-hCG, AFP, testicular ultrasound if markers elevated [OCC-4, OCC-8].

options.label: Treat as poor-risk germ cell tumor per NCCN Guidelines for Testicular Cancer or germ cell tumor per NCCN Guidelines for Ovarian Cancer OR treat per NCCN Guidelines for Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer
options.recommendation: Either approach is acceptable based on workup findings [OCC-4, OCC-8].

options.label: Treat per NCCN Guidelines for Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer
options.recommendation: Additional workup should exclude germ cell and breast primary [OCC-4, OCC-8].

management_pathways.title: Adenocarcinoma – Chest (multiple nodules) or pleural effusion
branching_basis: Clinical presentation: lung nodules (multiple) or pleural effusion

options.label: Consider surgery
options.recommendation: If completely resectable, surgical resection may be considered [OCC-9].

nodes.criteria: Lung nodules – not completely resectable or for any patient

options.label: Clinical trial preferred
options.recommendation: Enrollment in clinical trial is the preferred approach [OCC-9].

options.label: Consider systemic therapy
options.recommendation: Systemic therapy may be considered [OCC-B].

options.label: SBRT/SABR
options.recommendation: Stereotactic body radiation therapy (SBRT) or stereotactic ablative radiotherapy (SABR) can be used, especially for oligometastatic disease [OCC-9, OCC-C].

options.label: Symptom control
options.recommendation: Palliation of symptoms as needed [OCC-9].

nodes.criteria: Pleural effusion – Pathology defined (e.g., breast marker positive)

options.label: Treat as per appropriate NCCN Guidelines Treatment by Cancer Type
options.recommendation: If workup identifies a primary site, follow disease-specific guidelines [OCC-9].

nodes.criteria: Pleural effusion – Other (no tumor marker identified)

options.label: Clinical trial preferred
Consider systemic therapy
options.recommendation: Consider treating as lung primary (stage IVA) if not positive for another tumor marker per NCCN Guidelines for Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer [OCC-9].

options.label: Symptom control
Treat as per appropriate NCCN Guidelines Treatment by Cancer Type
options.recommendation: If primary identified, treat per disease-specific guidelines [OCC-9].

options.label: Clinical trial preferred
Consider systemic therapy
options.recommendation: Systemic therapy may be considered on an individual basis [OCC-9, OCC-B].

options.label: Symptom control

management_pathways.title: Adenocarcinoma – Retroperitoneal mass
branching_basis: Histology consistent with germ cell tumor vs non-germ cell

nodes.criteria: Histology consistent with germ cell tumor

options.label: Treat as poor-risk germ cell tumor per NCCN Guidelines for Testicular Cancer or germ cell tumor per NCCN Guidelines for Ovarian Cancer
options.recommendation: Additional workup includes beta-hCG, AFP, testicular ultrasound in patients with testes <65 years [OCC-5, OCC-9].

options.label: Surgery and/or RT
options.recommendation: Surgery and/or RT are recommended; consider systemic therapy for selected patients [OCC-9, OCC-C].

options.label: Lymph node dissection, consider RT if clinically indicated ± systemic therapy
options.recommendation: For unilateral inguinal adenopathy, lymph node dissection with consideration of adjuvant RT and/or systemic therapy [OCC-10].

adjuvant_logic.summary: Adjuvant RT may be considered if extranodal extension or inadequate nodal dissection with multiple positive nodes [OCC-C].

options.label: Bilateral lymph node dissection, consider RT if clinically indicated ± systemic therapy
options.recommendation: For bilateral inguinal disease, bilateral lymph node dissection; RT alone is category 2B [OCC-10].

adjuvant_logic.summary: Adjuvant RT may be considered with same criteria; RT alone is category 2B [OCC-C].

options.label: Surgical resection ± systemic therapy
options.recommendation: If surgery is medically contraindicated, treat as unresectable [OCC-10].

adjuvant_logic.summary: Systemic therapy may be considered as adjunct.

options.label: Treat as disseminated disease and/or consider locoregional therapeutic options
options.recommendation: See NCCN Guidelines for Hepatocellular Carcinoma for locoregional therapy options [OCC-10].

nodes.criteria: Isolated lesion or painful lesion or lesion with potential for fracture in weight-bearing area

options.label: Surgery for impending fracture (in patients with good PS) and/or RT
options.recommendation: If good PS, surgery and/or RT; if poor PS, RT alone [OCC-10, OCC-C].

options.label: See NCCN Guidelines for Central Nervous System Cancers for management of CNS metastatic lesions
options.recommendation: Primary treatment of CNS metastases per CNS guidelines [OCC-6, OCC-10].

management_pathways.title: Adenocarcinoma – Multiple sites of involvement / Disseminated metastases

nodes.criteria: Workup findings do not identify primary site

options.label: Symptom control
Clinical trial preferred
Consider systemic therapy on an individual basis
options.recommendation: Systemic therapy may be considered; base regimen on histologic cancer type [OCC-B]. For disseminated adenocarcinoma, preferred regimens include carboplatin/paclitaxel [1,2] and cisplatin/gemcitabine [3]. For presumed GI primary, CAPEOX [4], FOLFIRI [5-9], mFOLFOX6 [4,10] are preferred.

options.label: Specialized approaches
options.recommendation: Includes palliative care, targeted therapies, novel RT [OCC-7, Discussion].

management_pathways.title: Squamous Cell Carcinoma – Head and neck nodes
branching_basis: Clinical presentation: head and neck nodes

nodes.criteria: Additional workup per NCCN Guidelines for Head and Neck Cancers; check p16 IHC/HPV ISH and EBV ISH results

options.label: Treat per NCCN Guidelines for Head and Neck Cancers
options.recommendation: Follow the Occult Primary pathway in the NCCN Guidelines for Head and Neck Cancers [OCC-11, OCC-13].

nodes.criteria: Additional workup: neck/chest/abdomen/pelvis CT; endoscopy as indicated; check p16/HPV/EBV

options.label: Treat per NCCN Guidelines for Head and Neck Cancers
options.recommendation: Treatment per head and neck occult primary guideline [OCC-11, OCC-13].

nodes.criteria: Additional workup: chest CT; careful perineal and lower extremity exam; gynecologic oncologist consult; anoscopy; cystoscopy if indicated

options.label: Axillary node dissection, consider RT if clinically indicated ± systemic therapy
options.recommendation: For site-limited squamous cell carcinoma, axillary dissection with consideration of adjuvant RT and/or systemic therapy [OCC-11, OCC-13].

adjuvant_logic.summary: Consider RT if extranodal extension or inadequate nodal dissection [OCC-C].

options.label: Treat per NCCN Guidelines for Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer
options.recommendation: Management per NSCLC guidelines [OCC-11, OCC-13].

management_pathways.title: Squamous Cell Carcinoma – Multiple lung nodules or pleural effusion
branching_basis: Clinical presentation: multiple lung nodules or pleural effusion

options.label: Clinical trial preferred
Systemic therapy
options.recommendation: For squamous cell carcinoma, preferred regimens: carboplatin/paclitaxel [1,2], mFOLFOX6 [4,10]. Other recommended: capecitabine [11], carboplatin/docetaxel [41], cisplatin/gemcitabine [3], etc. [OCC-13, OCC-B 8 of 14].

options.label: Symptom control
Lymph node dissection, consider RT if clinically indicated ± systemic therapy
options.recommendation: For unilateral inguinal SCC [OCC-14].

adjuvant_logic.summary: Adjuvant RT may be considered for extranodal extension or inadequate dissection [OCC-C].

options.label: Bilateral lymph node dissection, consider RT if clinically indicated ± systemic therapy (category 2B for RT alone)
options.recommendation: Bilateral dissection; RT alone is category 2B [OCC-14].

adjuvant_logic.summary: Adjuvant RT may be considered; RT alone category 2B [OCC-C].

management_pathways.title: Squamous Cell Carcinoma – Bone
branching_basis: Isolated/painful/risk of fracture vs multiple lesions

nodes.criteria: Isolated lesion or painful lesion or bone scan–positive lesion with potential for fracture in weight-bearing area

options.label: Surgery for impending fracture (in patients with good PS) and/or RT
options.recommendation: If good PS, surgery and/or RT; if poor PS, RT alone [OCC-14, OCC-C].

options.label: Disseminated metastases (OCC-12)
options.recommendation: Treat as disseminated: symptom control, clinical trial preferred, consider systemic therapy [OCC-14].

options.label: See NCCN Guidelines for Central Nervous System Cancers for management of CNS metastatic lesions
options.recommendation: Primary treatment of CNS metastases per CNS guidelines [OCC-14].

management_pathways.title: Squamous Cell Carcinoma – Disseminated metastases
branching_basis: Clinical presentation: disseminated metastases (multiple sites, bone multiple lesions, etc.)

options.label: Symptom control
Clinical trial preferred
Consider systemic therapy on an individual basis
options.recommendation: Base regimen on histology; preferred regimens for squamous include carboplatin/paclitaxel [1,2] and mFOLFOX6 [4,10]. Other recommended include cisplatin/gemcitabine [3], carboplatin/gemcitabine [13], cisplatin/fluorouracil [43], etc. [OCC-12, OCC-B 8 of 14].

items.text: Complete history and physical (H&P) including breast, genitourinary, pelvic, rectal, skin, and/or oral cavity exam as appropriate, with attention to past biopsies or malignancies, removed lesions, spontaneously regressing lesions, existing imaging studies.
Complete blood count (CBC).
Comprehensive metabolic panel (CMP).
Lactate dehydrogenase (LDH) as indicated.
Chest/abdomen/pelvis CT scan (with contrast; MRI with and without IV contrast unless contraindicated; FDG-PET/CT is an alternative if contraindication to contrast enhancement).
Clinically directed endoscopy as indicated.
Core needle biopsy (preferred) and/or fine-needle aspiration (FNA) with cell block of most accessible site.
Consult pathologist for adequacy of specimen and additional studies including immunohistochemical (IHC) stains.
items.recommendation: Category 2A; see OCC-A for recommended markers.

items.text: Biomarker testing using next-generation sequencing (NGS) (or other technique to identify gene alterations or tumor mutational burden [TMB]).
items.recommendation: Category 2A; testing on tumor tissue and/or cell-free DNA is appropriate [g].

items.text: Microsatellite instability (MSI) or mismatch repair (MMR) testing.
items.recommendation: Category 2A; use IHC for MMR or PCR for MSI [h].

items.text: HER2 IHC.
Tissue of origin studies (gene expression profiling) are not recommended.
items.recommendation: Category 2A; based on data showing no clinical benefit in randomized trials [i].

pretreatment_evaluation.category: Additional Workup by Clinical Presentation – Adenocarcinoma

items.text: Predominant cervical nodes: See NCCN Guidelines for Head and Neck Cancers [OCC-3].

Supraclavicular/axillary nodes: Neck/chest/abdomen/pelvis CT; appropriate IHC; mammogram (if breast tissue or gynecomastia); if nondiagnostic and histopathologic evidence for breast cancer, breast MRI and/or breast ultrasound; PSA >40 y (if prostate present) [OCC-3].
Mediastinum: Chest/abdomen/pelvis CT; beta-hCG, AFP; appropriate IHC; mammogram; PSA >40 y; testicular ultrasound if markers elevated (if testes present) [OCC-4].

Chest (multiple nodules) or pleural effusion: Chest/abdomen/pelvis CT; appropriate IHC; consider gynecologic oncologist consult if CA-125 elevated or clinically indicated (if uterus/ovaries present); mammogram; PSA >40 y [OCC-4].

Peritoneal/ascites: Chest/abdomen/pelvis CT; urine cytology, cystoscopy if suspicious; appropriate IHC; CA-125 (if uterus/ovaries present); surgical or gynecologic oncologist consult; mammogram; PSA >40 y [OCC-4].

Retroperitoneal mass: Chest/abdomen/pelvis CT; urine cytology, consider cystoscopy if suspicious; appropriate IHC; gynecologic oncologist consult if CA-125 elevated (if uterus/ovaries present); mammogram; PSA >40 y; if <65 y and testes present: beta-hCG, AFP, testicular ultrasound [OCC-5].

Inguinal nodes: Chest/abdomen/pelvis CT; appropriate IHC; proctoscopy if clinically indicated; CA-125 (if uterus/ovaries present); gynecologic oncologist consult (if uterus/ovaries present); PSA >40 y [OCC-5].
Liver: Chest/abdomen/pelvis CT; endoscopic evaluation; AFP; appropriate IHC; mammogram; consider further liver-directed imaging [OCC-5].
Bone: Chest/abdomen/pelvis CT with bone scan; appropriate IHC; mammogram; PSA (if prostate present) [OCC-6].
Brain: See NCCN Guidelines for Central Nervous System Cancers; chest/abdomen/pelvis CT (if not done); appropriate IHC; mammogram [OCC-6].
Multiple sites: Chest/abdomen/pelvis CT; appropriate IHC; mammogram; PSA (if prostate present) [OCC-6].
Head and neck nodes: Head and neck workup per NCCN Guidelines for Head and Neck Cancers; check p16 IHC/HPV ISH and EBV ISH [OCC-11].
Supraclavicular nodes: Neck/chest/abdomen/pelvis CT; endoscopy as indicated; check p16/HPV/EBV [OCC-11].
Axillary nodes: Chest CT; careful perineal and lower extremity exam including reproductive organs and genitalia; gynecologic oncologist consult; anoscopy; cystoscopy if clinically indicated [OCC-11].
Inguinal nodes: Chest/abdomen/pelvis CT; careful perineal and lower extremity exam; gynecologic oncologist consult; anoscopy; cystoscopy if clinically indicated [OCC-11].
Bone: Bone scan (if only chest/abdomen/pelvis CT previously done); diagnostic imaging for painful lesions or bone scan–positive lesions with potential for fracture [OCC-11].

surgery.role: Surgery plays a role in diagnosis (biopsy) and treatment of localized occult primary cancers, particularly for lymph node dissection in inguinal and axillary presentations, resection of isolated metastases (e.g., lung nodules, liver lesions), and palliative surgery for impending fracture in weight-bearing areas. Surgery is also considered for resectable retroperitoneal masses with non-germ cell histology. The primary goal is locoregional control in favorable subsets.
principles: Core needle biopsy is preferred for initial diagnosis; FNA with cell block may be used if core needle biopsy is not feasible [OCC-1].
Surgical resection of isolated metastases (e.g., completely resectable lung nodules, liver lesions) can be considered when disease is limited and patient is medically fit.

Lymph node dissection (axillary, inguinal) is recommended for site-limited disease, especially for SCC; for bilateral inguinal involvement, bilateral dissection is recommended (RT alone is category 2B) [OCC-10, OCC-14].
For bone lesions with potential for fracture in weight-bearing areas, surgery for impending fracture is recommended in patients with good PS; otherwise RT alone is used [OCC-10, OCC-14].
If surgery is medically contraindicated for resectable liver lesions, treat as unresectable (disseminated metastases and/or locoregional therapy) [OCC-10].
Surgery may be considered for retroperitoneal mass with non-germ cell histology; RT can be used as alternative [OCC-9].

procedures.name: Lymph node dissection (axillary)
procedures.approach: Surgical excision of axillary lymph nodes.
procedures.indications: Squamous cell carcinoma with axillary node involvement; adenocarcinoma with axillary-limited disease (but then treat per breast cancer guidelines).
procedures.key_complications: Lymphedema, nerve injury, infection, seroma. (Not explicitly detailed in guideline, but standard surgical risks.)

procedures.name: Lymph node dissection (inguinal)
procedures.approach: Surgical excision of inguinal lymph nodes (unilateral or bilateral).
procedures.indications: Squamous cell carcinoma or adenocarcinoma with inguinal node metastasis; for SCC, dissection is recommended; for adenocarcinoma, unilateral or bilateral dissection as clinically indicated.
procedures.key_complications: Lymphedema, wound infection, vascular injury, nerve damage. (Standard surgical risks.)

procedures.name: Resection of lung nodules
procedures.approach: Surgical resection (e.g., wedge resection, segmentectomy, lobectomy) for completely resectable nodules.
procedures.indications: Adenocarcinoma or SCC presenting with multiple lung nodules if completely resectable; may be considered for oligometastatic disease.

procedures.name: Liver resection
procedures.approach: Surgical resection of isolated liver metastasis.
procedures.indications: Adenocarcinoma with resectable liver lesion; if surgery is contraindicated, treat as unresectable.
procedures.key_complications: Hepatic insufficiency, bleeding, bile leak, infection.

procedures.name: Surgery for impending fracture
procedures.approach: Orthopedic stabilization (e.g., intramedullary nailing, joint replacement) for lesions at risk of pathologic fracture.
procedures.indications: Adenocarcinoma or SCC with bone lesion(s) in weight-bearing area and good PS.

surgery.resectability_criteria: Resectability is determined by the extent of disease and patient fitness. For lung nodules, 'completely resectable' is used without further specification. For liver lesions, resectability is based on standard hepatic surgical criteria (not detailed in guideline). Surgery for impending fracture is reserved for patients with good performance status.

radiation_therapy.role: Radiation therapy (RT) is used for definitive treatment of localized disease (especially for limited metastases), as adjuvant therapy after lymph node dissection, and for palliation of symptomatic lesions (pain, impending fracture, cord compression). Stereotactic body RT (SBRT)/stereotactic ablative radiotherapy (SABR) is recommended for oligometastatic disease (1–3 metastases) and pulmonary metastases [OCC-C].
principles: Definitive radiotherapy can be considered for patients with active disease localized to a single nodal site or isolated metastasis.
Adjuvant RT after lymph node dissection is indicated if disease is limited to a single nodal site with extranodal extension or if there is inadequate nodal dissection with multiple positive nodes.
Palliative hypofractionated RT is used for uncontrolled pain, impending pathologic fracture, or impending cord compression.
Concurrent chemoradiation with capecitabine, fluorouracil, or cisplatin/fluorouracil can be given for selected presentations (e.g., presumed GI primary or SCC) [OCC-B 5,11].
SBRT/SABR is particularly recommended for limited (1–3) metastases based on SABR-COMET trial [1] and for pulmonary metastases [2].

dose_frameworks.name: Adjuvant RT (after lymph node dissection)
dose_frameworks.total_dose: 45 Gy with or without boost of 5–9 Gy
dose_frameworks.fractions: 22–25 fractions for 45 Gy; plus boost fractions as needed
dose_frameworks.indication: Isolated supraclavicular, axillary, or inguinal nodal metastasis with extranodal extension or inadequate dissection with multiple positive nodes.

dose_frameworks.name: Palliative hypofractionated RT
dose_frameworks.total_dose: 8 Gy (single fraction); 20 Gy (4–5 fractions); 30 Gy (10 fractions)
dose_frameworks.indication: Uncontrolled pain, impending pathologic fracture, impending cord compression.

approaches.name: Definitive RT (including SBRT/SABR)
approaches.dose_fractionation: 48–60 Gy in 4–5 fractions for oligometastases; other definitive regimens may be used for head and neck or mediastinal presentations per site-specific guidelines.
approaches.concurrent_chemotherapy: May be given with capecitabine (625–825 mg/m2 PO BID Days 1–5 weekly) or fluorouracil (200–250 mg/m2 CIV daily Days 1–5 weekly) for presumed GI primary; cisplatin/fluorouracil for SCC (cisplatin 75–100 mg/m2 Days 1 and 29 + fluorouracil 750–1000 mg/m2 CIV Days 1–4 and 29–32 with RT, or cisplatin 15 mg/m2 Days 1–5 + fluorouracil 800 mg/m2 CIV Days 1–5, 21-day cycle for 2 cycles with RT) [OCC-B 11].
approaches.indication: Limited nodal or extranodal disease, especially if unresectable or for organ preservation.
approaches.key_trial: SABR-COMET [1], Gomez et al. [2] for oligometastatic NSCLC.
approaches.toxicities: Fatigue, dermatitis, esophagitis, pneumonitis (depending on site). With concurrent chemotherapy, increased mucositis, hematologic toxicity.

approaches.name: Adjuvant RT
approaches.dose_fractionation: 45 Gy over 5 weeks to nodal basin ± boost 5–9 Gy.
approaches.concurrent_chemotherapy: Not routinely used; adjuvant systemic therapy may be given sequentially.
approaches.indication: After lymph node dissection for SCC or adenocarcinoma with extranodal extension or inadequate dissection.
approaches.key_trial: Not specifically reported in CUP; extrapolated from head and neck and other guidelines.
approaches.toxicities: Local skin reactions, fibrosis, lymphedema, xerostomia if head/neck region.

approaches.name: Palliative RT
approaches.concurrent_chemotherapy: Not typically used; systemic therapy may be given separately for symptom control.
approaches.indication: Symptomatic bone metastases, impending fracture, cord compression.
approaches.toxicities: Fatigue, local skin reaction, nausea (if large fields).

systemic_therapy.overview: Systemic therapy is indicated for symptomatic patients with PS 1–2 or asymptomatic patients with PS 0 and aggressive cancer. The choice of regimen is based on histologic cancer type (adenocarcinoma vs. squamous cell carcinoma) and, if available, biomarker results (MSI/MMR, TMB, BRAF, NTRK, RET, HER2). For disseminated disease, treatment is palliative with limited efficacy; enrollment in clinical trials is strongly preferred. Regimens are listed in categories of preference (Preferred, Other Recommended, Useful in Certain Circumstances) as per NCCN. For adenocarcinoma, preferred options include carboplatin/paclitaxel [1,2] and cisplatin/gemcitabine [3]; for presumed GI primary, CAPEOX, FOLFIRI, mFOLFOX6 are preferred. For squamous cell carcinoma, preferred options include carboplatin/paclitaxel [1,2] and mFOLFOX6 [4,10]. Biomarker-driven therapies (e.g., dabrafenib/trametinib for BRAF V600E, pembrolizumab for dMMR/MSI-H or TMB-H, entrectinib/larotrectinib/repotrectinib for NTRK fusions, selpercatinib for RET fusions, fam-trastuzumab deruxtecan for HER2 IHC 3+) are recommended for selected patients meeting specific indications [OCC-B].

by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: Carboplatin/Paclitaxel (AUC 5-6 IV Day 1 + paclitaxel 175-200 mg/m2 IV Day 1 q3w OR carboplatin AUC 2 IV Days 1,8,15 + paclitaxel 80 mg/m2 IV Days 1,8,15 q4w) [1,2]; Cisplatin/Gemcitabine (cisplatin 75 mg/m2 IV Day 1 + gemcitabine 1000-1250 mg/m2 IV Days 1 and 8 q3w) [3]
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Phase II/III data show ORR ~38-55% and median OS 8-11 months for these platinum-based regimens. Carboplatin/paclitaxel is well tolerated [140,150,151].
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Other Recommended: Carboplatin/Docetaxel [12], Carboplatin/Gemcitabine [13], Docetaxel/Gemcitabine [16], Irinotecan/Carboplatin [14]. Useful in Certain Circumstances: FOLFIRINOX/mFOLFIRINOX (PS 0-1, presumed GI primary) [34,36,37], Carboplatin/Etoposide/Paclitaxel (PS 0-1) [33], Gemcitabine/Irinotecan (platinum-ineligible) [35], Biomarker-driven options (see below).

by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: CAPEOX (capecitabine 850-1000 mg/m2 PO BID Days 1-14 + oxaliplatin 130 mg/m2 IV Day 1 q3w) [4]; FOLFIRI (irinotecan 180 mg/m2 IV Day 1 + leucovorin 400 mg/m2 IV Day 1 + fluorouracil 400 mg/m2 IV bolus then 1200 mg/m2/day CIV x 2 days q2w) [5-9]; mFOLFOX6 (oxaliplatin 85 mg/m2 IV Day 1 + leucovorin 400 mg/m2 IV Day 1 + fluorouracil 400 mg/m2 IV bolus then 1200 mg/m2/day CIV x 2 days q2w) [4,10]
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Based on colorectal cancer trials showing efficacy and tolerability; retrospective studies suggest benefit in CUP with colorectal molecular profile [167].
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Capecitabine single agent [11]; Roswell Park/Simplified/Weekly Fluorouracil/Leucovorin [17-19]; FOLFIRINOX/mFOLFIRINOX (PS 0-1) [34,36,37].

by_stage_and_setting.setting: Squamous cell carcinoma, first-line or subsequent (disseminated)
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Carboplatin/paclitaxel is commonly used for SCC of head and neck/lung; mFOLFOX6 based on esophageal SCC data [204,205].
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Other Recommended: Capecitabine [11], Carboplatin/Docetaxel [41], Carboplatin/Gemcitabine [13], Cisplatin/Docetaxel [15,42], Cisplatin/Fluorouracil [43], Cisplatin/Gemcitabine [3], Cisplatin/Paclitaxel [44], Roswell Park/Simplified/Weekly Fluorouracil/Leucovorin [17-19]. Useful in Certain Circumstances: Cisplatin/Docetaxel/Fluorouracil (PS 0-1) [47]; Biomarker-driven options (see below).

by_stage_and_setting.rationale: FDA-approved tissue-agnostic indications based on basket trials.

key_regimens.name: Carboplatin/Paclitaxel (q3w)
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Neutropenia, thrombocytopenia, neuropathy, fatigue, alopecia, allergic reactions [1].

key_regimens.name: Carboplatin/Paclitaxel (weekly)
key_regimens.setting: Adenocarcinoma and squamous cell carcinoma (first-line, alternative)
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Similar to q3w but possibly less neuropathy [2].

drugs.name: Fluorouracil
drugs.dose: 400 mg/m2 IV bolus then 1200 mg/m2/day CIV x 2 days

drugs.name: Fluorouracil
drugs.dose: 400 mg/m2 IV bolus then 1200 mg/m2/day CIV x 2 days

key_regimens.name: Pembrolizumab (dMMR/MSI-H or TMB-H)
key_regimens.setting: Adenocarcinoma and squamous – Useful in Certain Circumstances

drugs.name: Pembrolizumab
drugs.dose: 200 mg IV Day 1 OR 2 mg/kg IV Day 1 q3w; alternative 400 mg IV Day 1 OR 4 mg/kg IV Day 1 q6w

key_regimens.key_toxicities: Immunotherapy-related: pneumonitis, colitis, hepatitis, endocrinopathies, rash, fatigue [24-26,30].

drugs.name: Dostarlimab-gxly
drugs.dose: 500 mg IV Day 1 q3w for 4 doses, then 1000 mg IV Day 1 q6w

key_regimens.name: Dabrafenib/Trametinib (BRAF V600E)
key_regimens.setting: Adenocarcinoma and squamous – Useful in Certain Circumstances
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Pyrexia, fatigue, arthralgia, rash, nausea, diarrhea [22].

key_regimens.name: Entrectinib (NTRK fusion)
key_regimens.setting: Adenocarcinoma and squamous – Useful in Certain Circumstances

key_regimens.name: Larotrectinib (NTRK fusion)
key_regimens.setting: Adenocarcinoma and squamous – Useful in Certain Circumstances

key_regimens.setting: Adenocarcinoma and squamous – Useful in Certain Circumstances

drugs.name: Repotrectinib
drugs.dose: 160 mg PO once daily for 14 days, then 160 mg PO twice daily

drugs.name: Selpercatinib
drugs.dose: <50 kg: 120 mg PO twice daily; ≥50 kg: 160 mg PO twice daily

key_regimens.name: Fam-trastuzumab deruxtecan-nxki (HER2 IHC 3+)
key_regimens.setting: Adenocarcinoma and squamous – Useful in Certain Circumstances
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Nausea, fatigue, alopecia, neutropenia, interstitial lung disease [31].

treatment_response_assessment.title: Follow-up and Response Assessment for All Occult Primaries
treatment_response_assessment.timing: For patients with active disease or disease in remission, follow-up frequency should be determined by clinical need. For patients with no evidence of clinical disease, clinical follow-up with imaging every 6–12 months for up to 5 years is warranted [OCC-15].
response_logic: Response assessment is driven by clinical evaluation (history, physical exam) and imaging (CT scans as clinically indicated). The guideline does not specify formal response criteria (e.g., RECIST) but standard oncologic response assessment should be used.

For patients with active and incurable disease, psychosocial support, symptom management, end-of-life discussions, palliative care interventions, and hospice care should be considered and utilized as appropriate [OCC-15].
imaging_recommendations: Contrast-enhanced CT of chest/abdomen/pelvis is the primary imaging modality for follow-up. FDG-PET/CT may be used as alternative if contraindication to contrast or if needed for clarification [OCC-1A footnote d].
For patients with no evidence of clinical disease, imaging every 6–12 months for 5 years is recommended; after 5 years, imaging may be discontinued if no recurrence [OCC-15].
Diagnostic imaging studies for symptomatic lesions (e.g., bone scan for painful lesions, CT for suspected progression) should be performed as indicated.
biopsy_or_salvage_logic: The guideline does not provide specific recommendations for re-biopsy at progression. However, biomarker testing (NGS, MSI/MMR, HER2 IHC) is recommended at initial diagnosis; repeat biopsy may be considered to identify new actionable alterations if clinical progression occurs and tissue availability permits.
If a primary site is subsequently identified during follow-up (e.g., through imaging or clinical changes), treatment should be redirected per the appropriate disease-specific NCCN Guidelines [OCC-7].
